evidence_quotes.quote: All video games have similar mechanisms for gambling, e.g. the so-called ‘loot boxes’.
the forced withdrawal of access to digital products

paragraphs.text: I am most interested in the issues of search-friendly design and that my access to digital products such as video games is removed if, for example, the servers close, despite the fact that there is no need for an external service for the programme and I do not receive any financial compensation for my purchases. All video games have similar mechanisms for gambling, e.g. the so-called ‘loot boxes’. Lootboxes thus invest a digital currency in the video game and a random generator chooses between a large range of options of objects that can be finally obtained with different or small opportunities, but to get something about the guarantee. Two important facts need to be taken into account. First, many video games have digital currencies that can be bought with real money, and as the video game only accepts your own digital currency, it means that you can quickly lose an overview of how much you spend, e.g. I convert EUR 10 into 500 currency, and then buy a loot box that costs 375 at the end of which I only know 125 currency, but no more how many euros are. Secondly, the developer is very silent about the chances of winning the ‘better’ item, so many buy a lot of loot boxes, not because you don’t get anything, but because you don’t get anything good. It is acceptable to compare it with a gambling machine of at least EUR 5 and a guaranteed chance to give a ‘wine’, but nowhere does this machine say that with an opportunity of 90 %, 50 cents have a 9.9 % chance to win EUR 50 and a 0.1 % chance to win EUR 500. In the realistic Zenario, someone will repeat the very likely 50 cents and EUR 50 back into the machine in the hope of getting EUR 500 until the wallet is empty. Only for video games it is not the wallet but the bank account and the winnings do not in themselves have any financial value. This tactic is also used for video games that are suitable for customers under 18 years of age, e.g. the ‘FIFA’ series by the developer ‘Electronic Arts’ (Short EA) which gives a 0-year US score in the case of ‘FIFA 21’. And with regard to the forced withdrawal of access to digital products, which is supposed to function without external assistance, I can compare with the fact that I buy an old car without problems and without maintenance, but the manufacturer is no longer producing this model and therefore breaks in my garage and confiscated the car without at least returning my money. The same is also true for video games, for example in the Ubisoft titles ‘The Crew’, where the developer across the world removed access to the programme without warning or reimbursement for customers who had already paid for the game. There are video games that currently run online via the developer’s servers, but if the developer decides to close the servers, it has to open up the staff, the rescourcs and the knowledge to open up an update which allows customers to run the servers via their mobile hardware (play station, Xbox, desktop PC, etc.), thereby maintaining access and avoiding unnecessary money for the developer to keep the servers running.
